text: Derek's observation that Genesis genealogies parallel Deucalion's genealogy (Greek), that Noah parallels Deucalion as the first to plant a vineyard post-flood, and that Hebrew Bible narratives structurally mirror Homer's Iliad/Odyssey, extends the Greco-Christian framework argument beyond the New Testament to the Hebrew Bible itself. Critically, Derek notes: "Greeks were happy to say I got this from Bob the Bible authors never tell you they got it but it takes critical scholarships sifting through the material to see the antecedents." This pattern of unattributed Hellenistic influence on biblical composition is not directly addressed in the book but strengthens its overall thesis about pervasive Greco-Christian literary influence.
quote: take the genealogy in Genesis that genealogy of nationalistic individuals people that represent nations in a genealogical form post-flood like ducalian there's nowhere in Mesopotamia that has that...like you start going what's happening here

text: Lumpkkey demonstrates through multiple examples (Ifigenia/Jephthah, Jason/Medea parallels in Judges) that biblical narratives were composed using Greek literary patterns and mythological frameworks, supporting the book's argument that understanding Jesus traditions requires attention to Greek institutional and literary conventions rather than exclusively Judaic categories.
quote: And then judges then you have Samson... you have the same story and the bane of both people both heroes are women

text: MacDonald identifies the Judith narrative imitating Hera's seduction of Zeus (Iliad 14) and the Bacchae, showing that Hebrew Bible texts themselves imitated Greek epic to express Jewish political and theological claims. This demonstrates deep Greek-Jewish cultural integration at the literary level.
quote: we have a very famous jewish story that is imitating a homeric epic and an athenian play in order to um glorify the uh execution of holofernes

text: MacDonald's analysis of Moses as a "donor deity" similar to Dionysus and Demeter in Greek religion suggests that Jewish narratives consciously adapted Hellenistic theological concepts of divine authority and sustenance.
quote: moses striking the rock is similar to what you have in the bakay with uh striking a rock in the desert now then we have the relative dating of the bakkai and um the books of moses... moses becomes a donor deity in the wilderness supplying israel with water
